assumption_note: Dairy liquids (milk, cream, buttermilk, soft-serve mix before churning) run slightly denser than water due to fat/protein content -- ~8.6 lb/gal is a standard commonly cited dairy density. "CREAMER" added after finding it didn't match the word-bounded "\bCREAM\b" pattern (no boundary between "CREAM" and "ER").
Cooking oils are less dense than water -- ~7.5 lb/gal is a standard commonly cited figure for vegetable/corn/sesame oil.
High-sugar-content liquids (maple/corn syrup, honey, molasses) run notably denser than water due to dissolved sugar -- ~11 lb/gal.
Thick emulsified/high-solids condiments (mayonnaise, ketchup, mustard) run moderately denser than water -- ~9.0 lb/gal.

Catch-all for thinner liquids and solids-packed-in-brine (dressings, sauces, vinegar, wine, pickles, olives, relish, peppers, cabbage/kimchi, sauerkraut, horseradish) -- treated as close to water density (~8.3 lb/gal) since these are mostly water by content even when not a pure liquid. Verified with a $/lb check across the full GAL list: e.g. dressings/sauces in this bucket implied $1-3/lb, plausible for bulk foodservice condiments.
